name: Diomedes
description: Diomedes, son of Tydeus and Deipyle, was a prominent Achaean hero in the Trojan War, second only to Achilles in prowess according to some accounts. He was the King of Argos and a skilled warrior, renowned for his courage and strategic acumen on the battlefield. Diomedes was favored by Athena, who often intervened to aid him in battle, granting him superhuman strength and insight. His most notable exploits include wounding both Aphrodite and Ares in combat, a testament to his divine favor and martial skill. Beyond his military achievements, Diomedes represents the ideals of heroic virtue, piety, and loyalty, embodying the values prized by the ancient Greeks. Despite his fierce nature in battle, he was also known for his prudence and wisdom, acting as a trusted advisor to the other Achaean leaders. His legacy extends beyond the Trojan War, with various traditions and cults honoring him as a hero and protector of cities.
subtitle: King of Argos

keyMyths.title: Diomedes Wounds Aphrodite and Ares
keyMyths.description: During the heat of battle in the Trojan War, Athena empowered Diomedes with the ability to distinguish gods from mortals. He was instructed to attack Aphrodite should he encounter her aiding the Trojans. When Aphrodite attempted to rescue her son Aeneas, Diomedes, guided by Athena, struck her with his spear, wounding her. Later, he even dared to attack Ares, the god of war, also wounding him and forcing him to retreat from the battlefield. These acts demonstrate Diomedes' exceptional courage and divine assistance.

keyMyths.title: The Theft of the Palladion
keyMyths.description: The Palladion was a sacred statue of Athena believed to protect the city of Troy. An oracle prophesied that Troy could not fall as long as the Palladion remained within its walls. Diomedes, along with Odysseus, infiltrated Troy under the cover of darkness and successfully stole the Palladion. This daring act significantly weakened the Trojans' morale and contributed to the eventual Achaean victory. The story highlights Diomedes' bravery and his willingness to undertake perilous missions for the greater good of the Achaean cause.

keyMyths.title: Diomedes' Return and Exile
keyMyths.description: After the Trojan War, Diomedes' return to Argos was fraught with difficulty. Aphrodite, still bearing a grudge for being wounded, caused his wife Aegialia to be unfaithful. Upon his arrival, Diomedes faced betrayal and threats to his life. Eventually, he was forced to leave Argos and settled in Italy, where he founded several cities and continued to be revered as a hero. This myth underscores the consequences of angering the gods and the enduring impact of the Trojan War on the lives of its participants.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: The primary source for Diomedes' exploits during the Trojan War, detailing his battles and divine interactions.
